narrative:
High pressure is centered in the western Atlantic and extends into the Southeast. It's a warm, dry weekend. We'll be hard-pressed to find a finer weekend all summer. Get outside and make the most of it! Increasing moisture and a weak front approaching from the north produce daily isolated to widely scattered (mostly afternoon) showers and thundershowers next week.

The biggest meteorological anomaly this week is Hurricanes in Nevada. Go 'Canes!

2027 Blue Ridge Cameo Photo Winners Announced

In addition to our 15 winning photographs in the Blue Ridge Photo Contest, check out the 36 "Cameo Photo Winners" on the Photo Contest page .

RaysWeather.Com App

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

It's a warm, dry weekend. We'll be hard-pressed to find a finer weekend all summer. Get outside and make the most of it! Increasing moisture and a weak front approaching from the north produce daily isolated to widely scattered (mostly afternoon) showers and thundershowers next week. Subscribe Friday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion High pressure in the western Atlantic extends into the Southeast. It's pleasantly warm. Humidity levels are creeping up, but not enough yet to produce afternoon thundershowers. The exception could be SW VA tomorrow, but even there, the potential is not enough to "crack the forecast."   A weak backdoor front approaches Monday. It's fading off the map as it encounters a stubborn Southeastern ridge. Widely scattered afternoon thundershowers are expected. In a brief upslope flow, a few showers are possible later Monday night near the Blue Ridge (east). Tuesday is less warm with lots of clouds and stray, light showers in play.   Warm high pressure regains control of our weather for the rest of the next workweek but with more humidity in the Southern Appalachians. We will be dodging widely scattered mostly afternoon showers and thundershowers each day, Wednesday through Friday. Friday could have more "action" than the other two days.   Next weekend looks like a "splash and dash" weekend with a front from the NW reaching us early the following week. Submitted by Robert Maddix. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights May 2026 Recap Dry, Until It Wasn't The story of Spring 2026 was drought. Many stations experienced their warmest and/or driest April on record, and while the warmth backed off some into May, it took a while for the pattern to finally make a move towards the wetter side of things. But once it did, it did so with a bang. Not everywhere in the area made it to their respective May average precipitation numbers, due to the somewhat hit-or-miss nature of the rainy stretch around Memorial Day, but most recieved 70% or more of a month's worth of rainfall in about a week. No station took advantage of the wetter pattern more than the Asheville Airport, south of downtown. In a night that produced flash flooding damage to roadways around NE Henderson, SE Buncombe, and western Rutherford counties, the airport picked up nearly 3 inches of rain. 2.89", to be exact. That is almost as much as North Wilkesboro picked up for the entire month! As for KAVL's monthly total, it was an impressive 8.62", more than doubling average. It rained so much in the final 12 days of the month that the airport went from being over 8 inches behind normal annual accumulation on May 19th, to being just under 2 inches behind schedule. The graph below shows the numbers for the first 5 months of 2026 in green, as well as the wettest (blue) and driest (red) years, along with average (brown). In the High Country and SW Virginia, things were fairly hit-or-miss, with Boone, Beech Mountain, Wytheville, and Banner Elk all falling between 65 and 85 percent of average monthly rainfall, while Galax's 4.94" exceeded average by 24 percent. As mentioned earlier, the vast majority of this fell in roughly a 7 to 10 day stretch to end the month. In Boone's case, most of it fell in the days centered around the gloomy Memorial Day weekend. You can also see that warm stretch in the mid-month period before we finally made the switch to a wetter pattern, highlighted by four straight highs in the low 80s. Memorial Day Weekend Wedge As we get deeper into the summer, the classic Appalachian "wedge" becomes a more infrequent and less impactful phenomenon. However, late spring and summertime wedges are certainly not unheard of, and we had a doozy just before Memorial Day. Remarkably cool high temperatures invaded the Foothills on the 23rd, when many locations recorded a half inch to an inch of rain. The weather whiplash was strong in Wilkesboro, where three straight days in the 90s was suddenly interrupted by 80s, then 60s, then a high of just 55 degrees on the 23rd! The map below shows the daily rainfall for the 23rd. Record April to Average May If you recall from our April Recap, several locations in our region saw the warmest April on record. In Morganton, the average high temperature for the month of April was right under 80 degrees. What makes that number especially remarkable is that not only is it above average for the months of April and May, but that it actually was slightly higher than the average high temperature for May! In fact, the month-to-month increase in average high temperature from April to May should be about 7 or 8 degrees, depending on where you are. None of the sites listed in the tables below increased by more than 2.1 degrees, and four of them actually decreased (Lenoir, Marshall, Morganton, and North Wilkesboro). By and large, the final numbers for May were about as close to average as you will find. With the remarkable warmth of April being fairly close to May averages, we essentially had "two Mays" in 2026, weather-wise. Local Details The tables and maps below re-iterate the story told above, but at a more local scale. Only a handful of outliers exceed ~1.5F anomalies on average temperature for May. While there was plenty of variance in between, the washed-out average is almost exactly where it usually is.

As mentioned before, precipitation was a bit hit-or-miss with the switch over to summertime convective storms versus the widespread precipitation events of the cold season. However, most managed to get within 20% of normal or exceed it. The "feast or famine" is most obvious with the percentage differences from the Asheville Airport to Mount Airy: 203.7% vs 53.2%.

Jun 04, 2026 WWR? Carolina Movement Lab We did not have to travel far this week.... We got to interview our neighbors, Carolina Movement Lab! Jun 04, 2026 Monday Message for June 1, 2026 It's already June, and the first Monday Message of meteorological summer is available to subscribers. This week, cool transforms to warm, and the weather dries out. Also, we talk tropics! Jun 01, 2026 See all Golf-O-Meter 0 Golfballs Only ducks or polar bears. Good day for knitting. Maybe, if you are brave. Worth taking a chance. A good day to be outside. Bosses, you'd better stand at the door. (Or will bosses be first out the door?)
